items.q: Do all points on a stationary wave oscillate with the same amplitude?
items.correct: No — amplitude varies from zero at the nodes to a maximum at the antinodes.
items.misconception: Yes — amplitude is constant along the wave, as in a progressive wave.
items.random_wrong: No — amplitude is largest at the nodes.
items.why: Students transfer the constant-amplitude picture of progressive waves onto stationary waves.

items.q: When a wave refracts into a new medium, which property stays the same?
items.correct: The frequency — the speed and wavelength change together.
items.misconception: The wavelength stays the same and the frequency changes.
items.why: Students think frequency changes in glass, but frequency is set by the source; v = fλ adjusts through the wavelength.

items.correct: No — polarisation applies only to transverse waves, and sound is longitudinal.
items.misconception: Yes — any wave can be polarised with the right filter.
items.why: Students apply polarisation to all waves, missing that it requires oscillations perpendicular to the direction of travel.

items.q: Which type of wave can be polarised?
items.why: It's Transverse waves only. “Longitudinal waves only” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: At a node on a stationary wave:
items.misconception: The amplitude is at its maximum.
items.why: It's The displacement is always zero.. “The amplitude is at its maximum.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Two coherent sources interfere constructively at a point when the path difference is:
items.misconception: An odd number of half wavelengths
items.why: Correct answer: A whole number of wavelengths. “An odd number of half wavelengths” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A wave has frequency 50 Hz and wavelength 4 m. Using v = fλ, its speed is:
items.why: Correct answer: 200 m/s. “0.08 m/s” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A wave has frequency 250 Hz and wavelength 1.2 m. What is its speed?
items.why: Exact answer: 300 m/s. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
